verification.note: URL resolves to the canonical Google-hosted PDF (19 pages, title matches exactly: "Towards a future space-based, highly scalable AI infrastructure system design"). All 7 sub-claims for the method "Physics-based subsystem sizing (power/thermal/mass)" are supported by direct verbatim quotes: (1) "illustrative, planar 81-satellite constellation... Cluster radius is R=1 km"; (2) "mean cluster altitude of 650 km" + "dawn-dusk, sun-synchronous low-Earth orbit (LEO)"; (3) "Cooling would be achieved through a thermal system of heat pipes and radiators"; (4) "Google's V6e Trillium Cloud TPU... tested in a 67 MeV proton beam... continued to operate correctly up to the maximum tested cumulative dose of 15 krad(Si)... No hard failures were attributable to TID" and "HBM-specific stress tests began to show irregularities after a cumulative dose of 2 krad(Si)"; (5) "reaching <$200/kg by ~2035 would require... ~180 Starship launches/year"; (6) "could be ~$810/kW/y for a Starlink v2-type constellation... if launch drops to $200/kg" (Table 1 confirms Starlink v2 mini = $810/kW/y at $200/kg). Verdict confirmed stands. Note: WebFetch could not parse the PDF binary; verification was done by downloading the PDF (saved locally during fetch) and extracting text via PyMuPDF.

source.title: Why we should train AI in space (Starcloud / Lumen Orbit White Paper v1.03)
source.authors: Ezra Feilden, Adi Oltean, Philip Johnston
source.key_claim: 5 GW cluster on a ~4 km x 4 km solar array (90% fill, 22% Si BOL); radiator at 20C emits 385.24 W/m^2 per side (770.5 W/m^2 both sides) via Stefan-Boltzmann (epsilon=0.92); Earth/albedo absorption 14.46 W/m^2, direct-sun absorption 122.9 W/m^2 (alpha=0.09). Headline 40 MW/10 yr TCO: $8.2M space vs $167M ground, assuming $30/kg launch (Starship long-term, ~$5M/launch).
source.notes: Full PDF extracted. The $30/kg launch and ~$8M TCO are the most aggressive assumptions in the field; radiator double-sided 770 W/m^2 is a clean-plate ideal, not flight-demonstrated.

verification.note:
URL resolves to the canonical PDF: "Why we should train AI in space - White Paper v1.03, September 2024" by Lumen Orbit (now Starcloud), authors Ezra Feilden, Adi Oltean, Philip Johnston (13 pages, Google Docs renderer). WebFetch could not parse the FlateDecode streams, but I extracted the full text locally with pypdf and verified every figure against the source.

All claims confirmed verbatim:
1) 5 GW cluster / 4 km x 4 km array: "A 5 GW data center would require a solar array with dimensions of approximately 4 km by 4 km, assuming a cell fill factor of 90% and beginning-of-life efficiency of 22% using silicon solar cells." Exact match.
2) Radiator at 20C: "0.92 * 5.67 * 10^-8 * 293.15^4 = 385.24 W/m^2 ... a 1m^2 plate will emit 2 * 385.24 = 770.48 W (from both sides)." Emissivity 0.92 and Stefan-Boltzmann stated explicitly. Claim's "770.5" is the WP's 770.48 rounded. Match.
3) Earth/albedo absorption = 14.46 W/m^2 (alpha=0.09, view factor 0.25, albedo 0.3, S=1366); direct-sun = "0.09 * 1366 = 122.94 W/m^2." Claim's 122.9 = WP's 122.94 rounded. Match.
4) TCO Table 1, "Cost comparison of a single 40 MW cluster operated for 10 years in space vs on land," Cost Balance: terrestrial $167m vs space $8.2m. Exact match.
5) Launch: "reusable heavy-lift launchers ... expected to offer a launch price of around $5 million per launch long term. With a payload capacity of 100 tons to LEO/SSO, this translates to approximately $30 per kilogram." $30/kg also appears in the radiation-shielding cost line. Match.

Minor attribution nuance (non-distorting): the WP body text says "reusable heavy-lift launchers," not literally "Starship" (its cited source, ref 22, is an Elon Musk tweet). The claim's parenthetical "Starship long-term" is the verifier's reasonable gloss for a 100t-to-LEO reusable launcher at $5M/launch, not a verbatim quote. Does not affect the verdict.

Verdict: confirmed.

source.title: The Real Cost of Cooling GPUs in Space Might Shock You
source.authors: Andrew Cavalier (ABI Research), reported by IEEE Spectrum
source.key_claim: Stefan-Boltzmann sizing: one 700 W H100 needs ~1.4 m^2 radiator at 60C; a 40 kW rack needs ~80 m^2; a 100 MW facility needs >=2,500 such radiators. UV + atomic-oxygen degradation over a 5-yr LEO life raises required area by ~40%. Radiators+arrays = 65-70% of satellite mass. TCO at least an order of magnitude higher than terrestrial.
source.notes: Skeptical case. Confirms the 40% radiator degradation penalty and that the method's areas balloon at facility scale. Does NOT compare theory to ISS flight radiator data.

verification.note: URL resolves. IEEE Spectrum article by Andrew Cavalier (published 2026-06-11), actual title "Why Orbital Data Centers Are Harder Than Silicon Valley Thinks" (the cited title "The Real Cost of Cooling GPUs in Space Might Shock You" does not match, but URL and content do). Every numeric/technical element of the claim is supported by verbatim text: Stefan-Boltzmann sizing (power proportional to area x T^4); 700W H100 -> 1.4 m^2 radiator at 60C; 40 kW rack -> 80 m^2 ("size of a pickleball court"); 100 MW facility -> "at least 2,500 of those radiators"; UV+atomic-oxygen degradation over 5-yr LEO life raising area from ~1.4 to ~2.0 m^2/chip = "physics tax rises by 40 percent"; "Radiators and solar arrays can consume 65 to 70 percent of total satellite mass"; TCO "at least an order of magnitude higher than the same feat in a terrestrial data center." Verdict confirmed; only discrepancy is the article title, not the substance or URL.

source.title: ISS: ROSA (Roll Out Solar Array) / iROSA
source.key_claim: Each iROSA wing ~340 kg, 6 m x ~19.2 m deployed, >28 kW BOL (20+ kW sustained over 10 yr), specific power ~75.3 W/kg at wing/system level; six units added ~30% to ISS, total >250 kW.
source.notes: 75.3 W/kg cited in search aggregation and matches the 340 kg / 6x19.2 m geometry; eoPortal page itself states 20 kW/array and 20% mass reduction but does not print the 75 W/kg number verbatim. The flight-demonstrated ~75 W/kg sits well below the 110-350 W/kg specs used in optimistic ODC models.

verification.note:
The eoPortal URL resolves (HTTP 200, not a 404), but it does NOT support the claim. The page covers only the 2017 EXPERIMENTAL ROSA payload — a single 6 m x 13.7 m, ~20 kW test article that was jettisoned on 26 June 2017 after a failed retraction. It contains none of the eight operational iROSA figures in the claim. Verbatim page figures: "6 m x 13.7 m" deployed and "nominally 20 kW of electrical power per array" — note the dimension differs from the claimed 6 m x 19.2 m. The page never states 340 kg, >28 kW BOL, 20+ kW/10 yr, 75.3 W/kg, six units, ~30% increase, or >250 kW total.

The claimed numbers ARE factually correct for the OPERATIONAL iROSA arrays (verified via NASA, Redwire press releases, and Wikipedia): ~340 kg/wing, 6 m x 19.2 m, >28 kW BOL using 30.7% XTJ Prime GaAs cells, six units installed 2021-2023 (a 4th pair manifested), and combined ISS total >250 kW (~30% increase). So this is a source-mismatch: accurate facts cited to a page that does not contain them.

Verdict downgraded from 'partial' to 'refuted' because the cited source supports essentially none of the load-bearing figures (it describes a different, earlier hardware article), and per instruction to default harsher when support cannot be confirmed. Corrected/canonical source for the operational iROSA specs: the NASA STMD impact story (https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/stmd/impact-story-roll-out-solar-arrays/); Wikipedia 'Roll Out Solar Array' carries the six-unit and 120 kW augmentation figures but not the per-wing mass/specific-power numbers.

source.title: Space Datacenter TCO Model + 'To Boldly Go: The Case for Space Datacenters'
source.key_claim: Models launch-vehicle physics, thermal rejection limits, and GPU-level TCO 2026-2050; concludes orbital compute is ~4x terrestrial cost today, narrowing to ~30% premium by the early 2030s; cooling (radiative-only, no convection) is the largest structural constraint.
source.notes: Detailed model is paywalled; the ~4x-now / ~30%-by-2030s and 'cooling is the binding constraint' conclusions come from the public summary and secondary reporting (Semafor, WEF), not the raw model. This repo is calibrated to SemiAnalysis ('SA26').

verification.note: URL resolves; it is the canonical SemiAnalysis TCO-model landing page. The model page itself is partly gated, but its companion article (https://newsletter.semianalysis.com/p/to-boldly-go-the-case-for-space-datacenters) confirms every element of the claim with matching specifics: (1) models launch-vehicle physics + thermal rejection limits + GPU-hour TCO spanning 2026-2050; (2) base case "over 4x in 2026" (TCO 8.64 vs 2.37 $/hr/GPU ~3.6x; LCOC 10.91 vs 2.49 $/hr/GPU >4x); (3) explicitly "only ~30% more expensive" by the early 2030s, parity ~2040; (4) direct quote "Cooling is the largest structural constraint for orbital compute" with radiative-only/no-convection physics. The documented subsystems (solar array, Stefan-Boltzmann radiator hardware, cold plates/pumps/heat exchanger) match the cited method "Physics-based subsystem sizing (power/thermal/mass)." Claimed verdict of 'partial' is too harsh; corrected to 'confirmed'.

programs.name: Starcloud (Starcloud-1)
programs.target: Starcloud-1: single NVIDIA H100 GPU (~100x more GPU compute than previously flown), ~60 kg satellite. Vision: 5 GW orbital data center on a ~4 km x 4 km (16 million m2) solar/cooling array, modular 40 MW container-sized nodes by early 2030s. Claims up to ~10-22x lower energy cost vs terrestrial.
programs.status: DEMONSTRATED (small scale). Starcloud-1 launched 2 Nov 2025 on SpaceX; H100 operating in orbit at ~325 km. In Dec 2025 it ran a version of Gemini in space and trained Karpathy's nanoGPT LLM — first AI model trained in space. ANNOUNCED only: Starcloud-2 (full GPU cluster, ~100x power/compute, 2026) and the 5 GW vision. Raised $170M Series A (Mar 2026) at $1.1B valuation. Signed deal for 50+ Starlink Mini laser terminals. Bus mission life ~11 months then deorbits.
programs.timeline: Starcloud-1 flying since Nov 2025; Starcloud-2 targeted 2026; 5 GW array vision early 2030s
programs.note: Nvidia-backed. Largest real flight milestone to date for orbital AI compute. The 5 GW / 4km array is a long-range concept, not engineered hardware. Backup source: https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/starcloud-1-satellite-reaches-space-with-nvidia-h100-gpu-now-operating-in-orbit/

programs.name: Google Project Suncatcher
programs.target: Solar-powered LEO constellation of TPUs linked by free-space optical (laser) ISLs. Illustrative design: 81 satellites in a ~1 km-radius cluster at ~650 km, ~100-200 m inter-satellite spacing. Long-range: gigawatt-scale constellations. Uses Trillium (TPU v6e).
programs.status: PARTIALLY DEMONSTRATED on ground; flight not yet launched. DEMONSTRATED: Trillium TPU survived proton-beam radiation testing (HBM irregularities only after 2 krad(Si), ~3x the shielded 5-yr mission dose of 750 rad(Si); no hard failures to 15 krad(Si)); bench-scale optical link hit 800 Gbps each-way (1.6 Tbps total) on one transceiver pair. ANNOUNCED/MODELED: full constellation, orbital dynamics, thermal, economics. Learning mission with Planet Labs: two prototype satellites targeted early 2027.
programs.timeline: Research moonshot announced Nov 2025; two prototype sats with Planet targeted early 2027
programs.note: Framed explicitly by Google as a research moonshot, not a committed product. Solar panels in the right orbit ~8x more productive than on Earth. Backup: https://blog.google/technology/research/google-project-suncatcher/

programs.name: Thales Alenia Space ASCEND
programs.target: EU feasibility study (Advanced Space Cloud for European Net zero emission and Data sovereignty) under Horizon Europe. Studied environmental impact and technical feasibility of LEO space data centers vs terrestrial; aimed at EU Green Deal net-zero-by-2050 and data sovereignty.
programs.status: STUDY ONLY — no hardware flown. 18-month, EU-funded feasibility study; results revealed 27 Jun 2024. Concluded space DCs are technically feasible and environmentally favorable BUT contingent on a launcher ~10x less emissive over its lifecycle. No water cooling needed; projected multi-billion-euro ROI to 2050. Consortium: Thales Alenia Space, ArianeGroup, Airbus D&S, DLR, Orange Business, HPE, CloudFerro, Carbone 4, VITO.
programs.timeline: Study 2023-2024; results Jun 2024; no committed build timeline
programs.note: The most rigorous/independent feasibility assessment but purely a paper study. Key gating factor (10x cleaner launcher) does not yet exist. Backup: https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/thales-alenia-space-wins-eu-feasibility-study-for-ascend-space-data-centers/

programs.name: Lonestar Data Holdings
programs.target: Lunar / cislunar data storage (resilience & data sovereignty 'edge of Earth' archival). 'Freedom' payload: 8 TB SSD + single Microchip PolarFire SoC FPGA (with Phison). Roadmap: multi-petabyte purpose-built storage operating from the Earth-Moon L1 Lagrange point.
programs.status: DEMONSTRATED (storage payload, lunar transit/surface). 'Freedom' launched 26 Feb 2025 on Falcon 9 aboard Intuitive Machines' Athena (IM-2) lander; completed commercial operational tests in cislunar space (5 Mar 2025) over ~300,000 km. ANNOUNCED: commercial services 2026; first L1 multi-petabyte data center 2027.
programs.timeline: Freedom flew Feb-Mar 2025; commercial service 2026; L1 nodes from 2027
programs.note: This is data STORAGE/archival, not AI compute at scale — different thesis from the GPU/TPU programs. Note IM-2 Athena lander itself tipped over on the lunar surface; the data-center value prop was the cislunar/transit demonstration. Backup: https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/lunar-data-center-achieves-first-success-en-route-to-the-moon-302392544.html

programs.name: Axiom Space Orbital Data Center Nodes
programs.target: Optically interconnected, high-performance ODC nodes in LEO, initially hosted on/around the ISS, to let satellites, spacecraft, astronauts and researchers store/process data and run AI/ML and cloud workloads at the edge. Partners: Spacebilt, Skyloom (optical comms terminal), Phison, Microchip. Targets national-security, commercial and international customers.
programs.status: DEMONSTRATED (prototype + first nodes flown). AxDCU-1 (shoebox-size demonstrator) launched to ISS Aug 2025 to prove on-orbit edge hosting of hybrid-cloud/cloud-native apps. First two Axiom Orbital Data Center Nodes launched to LEO 11 Jan 2026. ANNOUNCED: at least three nodes running by end of 2027.
programs.timeline: AxDCU-1 Aug 2025; first 2 nodes Jan 2026; 3+ nodes by end 2027
programs.note: Edge-compute/data-relay scale, not gigawatt AI training. Open risk: ISS retirement (~2030) raises questions about ISS-hosted nodes' longevity. Backup: https://www.datacenterknowledge.com/next-gen-data-centers/iss-data-center-launch-tests-edge-computing-at-400km-above-earth

programs.name: SpaceX (AI satellites / orbital data center megaconstellation)
programs.target: Most ambitious by scale. FCC filing proposes a constellation that, at ~1 million tonnes of satellites launched per year, would generate ~100 GW of AI compute. AI1 satellite: 120 kW nominal / 150 kW peak compute payload, interchangeable chip payload, spans wider than a Boeing 747. Musk targets ~1 GW/yr of space AI compute by end of 2027, scaling to ~100 GW/yr by 2030, with terawatt-level aspirations. New 11M-sq-ft 'Gigasat' factory (>10x Starfactory).
programs.status: ANNOUNCED / EARLY (no AI compute satellite flown yet). FCC filing dated 30 Jan 2026; Gigasat factory unveiled; substantial AI-satellite production targeted before end of 2027. Leverages existing Starlink V3 tech (lasers, power, mass production). xAI acquired by SpaceX (Feb 2026); reported $920M/month compute deal with Google. DEMONSTRATED today: only the underlying Starlink platform, not orbital AI data centers.
programs.timeline: FCC filing Jan 2026; ~1 GW/yr by end 2027; ~100 GW/yr by 2030 (stated goals)
programs.note: Targets dwarf all others but are entirely forward-looking; depends on Starship cadence and Gigasat ramp. Treat GW/TW figures as ambition, not capability. Constellation/factory backups: https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/spacex-files-for-million-satellite-orbital-ai-data-center-megaconstellation/ and https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/big-tech/spacex-unveils-11-million-square-foot-gigasat-factory-a-new-manufacturing-facility-for-space-based-data-centers-aims-for-1-gw-year-of-space-ai-compute-by-late-2027-from-its-satellites

ambitious.summary:
Survey of the most ambitious orbital/space data centre programs as of mid-2026, ordered roughly by ambition and maturity. Honest demonstrated-vs-announced split is the key takeaway: real hardware is flying, but only at small scale; every gigawatt-class claim is still a concept.

WHAT IS ACTUALLY DEMONSTRATED IN SPACE (small scale):
- Starcloud-1: first NVIDIA H100 in orbit (launched 2 Nov 2025); first AI model trained in space (Karpathy nanoGPT) and ran Gemini, Dec 2025. ~60 kg sat at ~325 km, ~11-month life. This is the headline real-world milestone for orbital AI compute.
- Lonestar 'Freedom': lunar/cislunar data STORAGE payload (8 TB SSD + PolarFire FPGA) flew Feb-Mar 2025 on IM-2 Athena; storage thesis, not AI compute.
- Axiom: AxDCU-1 prototype to ISS (Aug 2025); first two Orbital Data Center Nodes to LEO (11 Jan 2026). Edge-compute/data-relay scale.

GROUND-DEMONSTRATED, FLIGHT PENDING:
- Google Project Suncatcher: Trillium (TPU v6e) passed proton-beam radiation testing; bench optical link hit 800 Gbps each-way (1.6 Tbps). Two prototype sats with Planet targeted early 2027. Explicitly a research moonshot. Illustrative design: 81 sats in a ~1 km cluster at 650 km.

PAPER / ANNOUNCED ONLY:
- Thales Alenia ASCEND: EU Horizon feasibility study (results Jun 2024) — deemed feasible BUT only if a launcher ~10x less emissive exists; no such launcher today; no hardware.
- SpaceX: by far the largest TARGETS — FCC filing (30 Jan 2026) for a megaconstellation implying ~100 GW of AI compute; AI1 sat at 120 kW (150 kW peak); ~1 GW/yr by end 2027, ~100 GW/yr by 2030; new Gigasat factory. Nothing flown yet; rides on Starlink V3 + Starship.
- Starcloud's own long-range 5 GW / ~4km x 4km array vision is similarly aspirational.

BOTTOM LINE: As of 2026 the field has crossed from pure concept into real (kilowatt-to-low-tens-of-kW-class) flight demonstrations — Starcloud is the standout for AI compute. The gigawatt-to-terawatt narratives (SpaceX, Starcloud 5 GW, Suncatcher GW-scale) remain unbuilt and gated on cheap heavy-lift launch, in-space thermal rejection at scale, and radiation-tolerant high-density compute. ASCEND's launcher-emissions finding is the cleanest statement of the core gating constraint. Caveats: some figures (e.g., exact AI1 chip vendor, precise Starcloud-2 specs) are not firmly disclosed in primary sources and are flagged as such per program.

critiques.point: Turyshev (JPL) independent mass/cost analysis: a PV + storage + radiator orbital data centre has a base mass-to-power ratio of 29.4 kg/kW, rising to 34-59 kg/kW once fixed spacecraft mass (structure, avionics, comms, propulsion) is included. At ~40 kg/kW, the terrestrial infrastructure benchmark of 10-40 k$/kW leaves only 250-1000 $/kg for combined launch + spacecraft build before comms, ops, utilization and lifetime are even counted. That allowance is 3.4-13.5x below the current Falcon 9 dedicated LEO launch price ALONE, before the spacecraft is built.
critiques.quantitative_detail: 29.4 kg/kW base; 34-59 kg/kW with fixed mass; budget of only 250-1000 $/kg vs Falcon 9 price 3.4-13.5x higher; 1 MW node needs 5.64e3 m2 PV (BOL) and 2.50e3 m2 radiator. Turyshev concludes terrestrial general-purpose compute 'closes only' under very restrictive conditions (low Earth-coupled comms intensity, high utilization, long lifetime, very low launch+build cost); viable near-term use is space-native preprocessing / comms-integrated edge compute, not bulk AI training.
critiques.note: This is the named source in the prompt and it directly substantiates the 34-59 kg/kW figure. arXiv ID 2604.27197 (April 2026). Strongest single skeptical source. The economics conclusion is genuinely damning for the 'orbital AI training cluster' thesis.

critiques.point: FCC 5-year deorbit rule vs ~15-year terrestrial facility life mismatch. The FCC's 5-year rule (adopted Sept 2022, effective for applications after Sept 29, 2024) requires LEO satellites below 2000 km to deorbit within 5 years of mission end, down from the old 25-year guideline. A terrestrial data centre is financed and depreciated over ~15+ years with continuous hardware refresh; an orbital asset forced to deorbit on a 5-year horizon must amortize its entire CapEx over roughly one-third the life with no mid-life refresh.
critiques.quantitative_detail: FCC 5-year deorbit horizon vs ~15-year terrestrial facility depreciation life — a ~3x amortization-window penalty. The 5-year figure is also independently reinforced by solar/electronics radiation degradation limiting practical satellite life to ~5 years.
critiques.note: Both halves are individually confirmed: the FCC rule is real and the 5-year number is exact; the 15-year terrestrial life is the standard industry depreciation horizon. Marked PARTIAL because no single source frames them as a head-to-head 'mismatch' — that synthesis is the analyst's, and the FCC rule technically counts from mission END, so a satellite could operate >5 years then deorbit. The economic substance (short amortization window, no refresh) holds and is reinforced by the radiation-degradation 5-year-life point from the TechCrunch and Turyshev sources.

critiques.point: Financeability of non-repossessable, non-serviceable assets raises WACC. A satellite cannot be repossessed in any practical sense, cannot be remarketed or liquidated, and has no robust secondary market — lenders treat a security interest in it as 'theoretical at best: inaccessible, uncontrollable, and ultimately unreliable as recourse.' Unlike aircraft/ships (where asset-based finance is mature), banks are hesitant to lend against satellites as primary collateral, pushing deals toward limited-recourse project finance and raising the cost of capital.
critiques.quantitative_detail: Qualitative/legal confirmation that satellites are non-repossessable, illiquid collateral with no secondary market, forcing project-finance structures. No public source provides a specific WACC delta (e.g. '+X% vs terrestrial DC').
critiques.note: The core claim — non-repossessability undermining collateral value and pushing financing toward higher-cost structures — is solidly confirmed by aviation/space finance legal sources. The specific WACC quantification the prompt asks for is NOT available in public sources, so the *number* is unverifiable even though the *mechanism* is real. Honest framing: directionally correct, not quantified in the literature.

critiques.point: Space-insurance market capacity is far too small to cover an orbital data centre fleet. The entire global space insurance market collects only ~$500-600M in premiums/year and insures ~$25B of assets annually. Single-launch capacity in 2025 was ~$300-325M, and even at the satellite level coverage tops out around ~$500M per risk. A single ~1 GW orbital DC is estimated at ~$42.4B of CapEx — roughly 130x the per-launch insurance capacity and ~1.7x the entire annual insured asset base of the whole industry.
critiques.quantitative_detail: Market premium ~$500-600M/yr; ~$25B insured assets/yr; per-launch capacity ~$300-325M, per-satellite ~$500M; 2023 losses ~$1B caused capacity contraction. Against a ~$42.4B orbital DC, insurance capacity is ~2 orders of magnitude short. Insurers' risk appetite already restricted after 2023 losses.
critiques.note: Market-size and per-risk-capacity figures are confirmed across multiple insurance-industry sources. The $42.4B orbital DC CapEx comes from the TechCrunch/SemiAnalysis economics piece. The conclusion that current insurance capacity cannot cover such an asset is a robust, well-supported critique.

critiques.point: Radiator heat-rejection (~W/m2) is not flight-demonstrated at the required scale. Space radiators reject only ~350-450 W/m2 (roughly equal to the ~400 W/m2 solar collection density). A 1 GW DC at optimistic 40% efficiency dumps ~600 MW of waste heat, needing on the order of ~834,000 m2 of radiator; a single 40 kW AI rack alone needs an ~80 m2 radiator. No large-scale (hundreds-of-kW to MW) deployable space radiator has been flight-demonstrated — the largest flown analogues are JWST-style origami radiators with no published large-scale performance data.
critiques.quantitative_detail: ~450 W/m2 heat rejection (~400 W/m2 solar in); 40 kW rack -> ~80 m2 radiator ('pickleball court'); 100 MW DC -> 2,500+ such radiators; 1 GW @40% eff -> ~600 MW heat -> ~834,000 m2 radiator. Degradation over 5 yr raises per-chip area ~40% (1.4 -> ~2.0 m2 at 60C). No MW-class deployable radiator flight-proven.
critiques.note: Heat-rejection density and area figures confirmed by IEEE Spectrum and WEF. The 'not flight-demonstrated at scale' claim is confirmed by absence: sources discussing MW-scale thermal management treat it as a future R&D challenge ('expected to intensify'), and no flown system approaches the required area. WEF explicitly calls cooling 'the real obstacle.'

critiques.point: iROSA flight reality (~33-75 W/kg) undercuts optimistic ROSA specs. iROSA on the ISS achieves a measured ~75 W/kg (≈340 kg per wing, ~20+ kW, 6m x 19.2m per the optimistic figures), but mass-conservative sources put the assembly at ~600 kg per ~20 kW unit, implying only ~33 W/kg. Either way, real flight specific power is far below the >150-200 W/kg often assumed in orbital-DC mass budgets, and silicon cells degrade faster in space radiation, cutting BOL power over the ~5-year life.
critiques.quantitative_detail: iROSA: ~75.3 W/kg using ~340 kg / 6mx19.2m / 20+ kW; but ~33 W/kg if mass is ~600 kg/unit per skyrocket.de. Original ROSA: 325 kg, '20% lighter, 1/4 the volume' of rigid arrays. ISS legacy arrays designed for 15-yr life and have degraded. No clean published 'spec vs flight' delta.
critiques.note: PARTIAL because the 75 W/kg figure is well-supported (matches the prompt's ROSA-spec number) but the mass basis is inconsistent across sources (340 kg vs ~600 kg per unit), so the *true* flight specific power sits in a 33-75 W/kg band rather than a single verified value. The qualitative point — flight arrays underperform aggressive mass-budget assumptions and degrade in radiation — is confirmed. The precise 'iROSA 75 vs ROSA spec' shortfall claim is not cleanly documented as a spec-miss in public sources.

critiques.point: On-orbit servicing/repair capability gaps. Commercial servicing exists only at small scale: Northrop Grumman's MEV-1 (2020) and MEV-2 (2021) are the only commercial GEO life-extension dockings to date, with the first commercial GEO undocking achieved April 2025. More capable robotic servicing (Mission Robotic Vehicle with DARPA arms for repair/upgrade/refueling) is still pre-flight, with four demo missions only planned for 2026. There is no demonstrated capability to refresh GPUs or repair compute hardware in LEO at fleet scale.
critiques.quantitative_detail: Only 2 commercial life-extension dockings ever (MEV-1 2020, MEV-2 2021), both GEO station-keeping not repair; first commercial undocking Apr 2025; dexterous robotic servicing (MRV) not yet flown, 4 demos planned 2026. Zero demonstrated on-orbit compute-hardware refresh/repair.
critiques.note: Confirmed. Existing servicing is GEO life-extension 'tugs', not the component-level repair or hardware refresh an orbital DC would need. The capability the orbital-DC thesis assumes (refresh/repair in LEO) is non-existent today and the nearest demonstrations are still planned, not proven.

critiques.point: Debris / collision risk in LEO is rising and threatens dense orbital DC constellations. A 2025 study finds the intact-object population already exceeds the Kessler runaway threshold at nearly all altitudes between 520-1,000 km. Starlink alone now performs a collision-avoidance maneuver roughly every 2 minutes on average; ESA reports LEO collision risk up ~20% into 2026. SpaceX's Jan 2026 filing for up to 1,000,000 additional satellites for orbital DCs would exceed all other operators combined, concentrating high-value, hard-to-service assets in the most congested shells.
critiques.quantitative_detail: Runaway threshold exceeded 520-1000 km; ~8,000 active satellites in the 500-600 km band; Starlink ~1 avoidance maneuver / 2 min; ESA LEO collision risk +20% in 2026; SpaceX filing for up to 1M new satellites. A non-serviceable orbital DC sitting 5+ years in this environment carries elevated, uninsurable-at-scale collision exposure.
critiques.note: Confirmed by ESA's environment report, an arXiv conjunction study, and IEEE Spectrum. The Kessler-threshold and maneuver-frequency figures are striking and directly relevant: an orbital DC must survive years in the busiest orbital shells with no repair option. The 1M-satellite figure is a SpaceX filing (intent), not a deployment.

critiques.point: Downlink/data-egress bottleneck. The industry already faces a 'downlink deficit': pLEO data generation outpaces ground capacity. Best demonstrated optical downlink is ~200 Gbps (NASA TBIRD); lasercom links suffer atmospheric outages limiting per-station availability to only ~50-60% even at good sites. To behave as one data centre, satellites need 10 Tbps+ inter-satellite links — roughly 50x the best demonstrated space-to-ground rate — to mimic an AI supercomputer's internal fabric. Egress of training-scale datasets to/from orbit is a hard, unsolved constraint.
critiques.quantitative_detail: Best demonstrated optical downlink ~200 Gbps (TBIRD); 100 Gbps coherent at LEO tracking rates (Nature 2022); lasercom availability ~50-60% per ground site due to weather; orbital-DC fabric needs 10 Tbps+ ISLs (~50x gap to ground). RF constrained by spectrum.
critiques.note: Confirmed across NASA, Nature, and cailabs/Archangel sources. The Turyshev paper independently flags 'communication intensity' (downlink per unit of IT energy) as a primary viability gate, reinforcing that egress — not just compute — is the binding constraint for any non-edge workload. Strong, well-documented critique.

skeptic.summary: From a space-systems-engineering standpoint, the 2026 skeptical case against orbital data centres is strong and mostly well-documented. The anchor source is Turyshev (JPL, arXiv 2604.27197, April 2026), an independent first-principles analysis finding a mass-to-power ratio of 29.4 kg/kW base, rising to 34-59 kg/kW with fixed spacecraft mass. At ~40 kg/kW the terrestrial 10-40 k$/kW benchmark leaves only 250-1000 $/kg for launch+build — 3.4-13.5x below Falcon 9's launch price alone. He concludes bulk terrestrial-equivalent compute 'closes only' under very restrictive conditions; only space-native edge/preprocessing is near-term viable. Independent economics (TechCrunch/SemiAnalysis) put a 1 GW orbital DC at ~$42.4B (~3x ground) and orbital energy at ~$14,700/kW-yr vs $570-3,000 terrestrial.\n\nOf the nine critiques requested: five are fully CONFIRMED with hard numbers (Turyshev mass/cost; space-insurance capacity ~$500-600M premium / ~$300-325M per launch vs a $42.4B asset; radiator ~450 W/m2 with ~834,000 m2 needed for 1 GW and no MW-scale flight demo; on-orbit servicing limited to 2 commercial GEO dockings with repair still pre-flight; debris/Kessler threshold already exceeded 520-1000 km with Starlink maneuvering ~every 2 min; downlink capped at ~200 Gbps demonstrated vs 10 Tbps+ needed). Three are PARTIAL — the mechanism is real but the specific number the prompt cites isn't cleanly documented in public sources: the FCC 5-year vs 15-year 'mismatch' (both halves true, but the FCC clock starts at mission end and no source frames it as a direct mismatch); the WACC penalty on non-repossessable assets (non-repossessability confirmed by space-finance law, but no published WACC delta exists); and iROSA specific power (well-supported ~75 W/kg, but mass basis varies 340 vs ~600 kg/unit giving a 33-75 W/kg band, and it's not documented as a formal spec-miss). No critique was refuted. Net: the engineering and economic skepticism is justified and largely quantified; the two genuinely unquantified-in-public claims are WACC and a clean iROSA spec-vs-flight delta. All source URLs are in the per-critique entries.

summary: Web-backed literature search of orbital-data-centre modelling methods, with adversarial citation verification
